entry.title: Re: General Availability of Google Big Query Live Data Integration in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Mustafa,

 

This is not about replicating SAP master and fact data into GBQ, but rather for scenarios where data is in a hyper-scalar data lake (in this case, GBQ) and the customer does not want to bother with replication into SAC of BDC.  This allows you to run your SAC semantic data models directly off of fact data in the data lake.  

 

In BI scenarios, "actuals" does not mean anything more than "the fact data".  It becomes important in planning scenarios, as there can be different versions of fact data.  The actuals is the single point of truth, backward looking BI fact data.  Planning models can support other versions as well, with forward looking projections, forecasts and plans.  The general idea is to leave the actuals in the original location and only have the plan version fact data replicated into SAC.

entry.title: SuccessFactors Joule - Transaction, Navigational, RBP, Groups - How is this connected?
entry.content:
Welcome, SuccessFactors Admins!

If you’re reading this, you’re likely curious about how Joule can enhance your SuccessFactors experience and how SF Permissions and group assignments impact its use cases. This blog post is designed to provide you with a comprehensive understanding of Joule and its implications for both navigational and transactional use cases within SuccessFactors.

Through this post, we’ll delve into the fundamentals of Joule, explore the relationship between SF Permissions and Joule’s capabilities.

 

~~~~~~ Navigational Use Cases ~~~~~~ 
Navigational Use Cases helps your end users to navigate through the systems (incl. SuccessFactors). It can provide information to the end user but mostly they provide LINKS to applications.

But how does Joule knows for what SF applications you have permissions? Joule reacts in RBP and based on the RBP we know that deep links are allowed for the end user.

Let us have a look to an example and let us pick a pure navigational case: "Show the org chart".

How it should look like:

Here we have a pure navigation - so based on my permissions Joule knows I'm allowed to open the org chart application and creates the link.

RBP's I need for this:

 

In the background - via the Refresh Synthetic Group Data Job, this user is assigned to a specific deep link permission group.

 

You can find this deep link groups here (Data Inspector -> Entry: Table_Users_Group)

 

In this list we are able find one group that indicates the access to the org chart "deepLinkActivationPermission_org_chart_view_org_chart"

 

As you can see on the screen shot this deep link role is assigned to 520 users.

The technical ID of this group is 7953 on this demo system.

 

During the Joule set up you have implemented an IPS Synch Job that synchs users and user assignments to WorkZone. In the settings for this IPS Job we have a filter in our source system to select only groups that are related to the deep link groups.

With this setting we makes sure, that we send only this group assignments to WorkZone (for Joule) that are relevant for deep link and navigation.

After running the Synch Job you can find the User-Group assignment in WorkZone. And here we should be able to find our group Id for the org chart deep link.

If I check my user, you can see a lot of assigned groups in WorkZone:

 

And here we see also our SuccessFactors Group for the org chart deep link:

 

Because we have the permission for this deep link, Joule shows us the link.

 

Let us have a look to an other example and let us pick a navigational case that also provides information: "Show my manager”.

This use case is a combination of:

- show information based on API Response

- generate the deep link to the user profile based on deep link permissions

 

What I often see is the following result:

As you can see, we see the name of the manager (API based) but not the button to navigate to the profile.

 

In this case the navigation them self is not established correctly.

So even the permission to access people profile is missing or the refresh synthetic group date job and/or the IPS job has not yet transferred the permission.

So to double check what is wrong, to the following checks:

  1. make sure the user has RBP based permission to people profile
  2. check what Group ID is generated for the people profile
  3. check if your user has this group assigned in WorkZone

 

In case the group is not assigned but RBPs are ok, re-run the SF Job and the IPS Job the get the user updated.

In case the group is assigned, and you can find it, the navigation should work.

 

~~~~~~ Transactional Use Cases ~~~~~~ 

Transactional Use Cases allows your users to start transactions and process to generate, change, maintain data. Here we do not use this deep link groups. we stick to the APIs we are calling and to the results of the APIs.

We have the most transaction Use Cases in EC - please have a look to the blog post SuccessFactors Joule - Joule and EC Quick Actions - SAP Community to understand how EC, Quick Actions and EC RBPs are connected to Joule.

Here I want to highlight the Platform related transactional use cases to explain the relationship between Joule results and RBP. Please have a look to the overview of Platform Use Cases | SAP Help Portal.

Let us pick one "Show my department".

Show department is a pure platform use case (not an EC use case). So, we try to fetch the data from the Live Profile User / Employee Data NOT from JobInformation. Means, users need permission to see the department via Employee Data.

Please have in mind: In case you have EC the information from the JobProfile is synched via HRIS Synch to the Employee Data.

 

In case have the permission, Joule will answer with my department details.

 

In case we do not have permissions, Joule will tell you, that Joule can't help because of missing configuration or permission. This is a typical answer in case the API response is empty.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: We want to assign SAP_ALL but remove TR, movement
entry.content:
Hello,

 

this is not that hard, as it seems.

First, identify the authorizations, which you do not want to be contained (STAUTHTRACE/ST01).
Once you know, what you want to exclude, Create a role from scratch. Either enter authorizations from profile SAP_ALL or use the SAP_ALL template.

Then remove the unwanted authorizations or set them to 'inactive'.

Generate the profile and assign the role to the corresponding user.

Done.

 

All steps shall be easily to be performed by a role administrator. This is no miracle.

 

b.rgds, Bernhard

entry.title: Semantic Querying with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph using RDF, SPARQL, and Generative AI in Python
entry.content:
SAP Knowledge Graph is now generally available (Q1 2025) and is poised to fundamentally change how data relationships are mapped and queried. With grounded intelligence, knowledge graphs are crucial in enabling AI agents for reasoning and retrieval with context and high accuracy. SAP Knowledge Graph Engine includes a native RDF triplestore and supports SQL/SPARQL interoperability, allowing both semantic and relational access. You can import/export RDF file formats, including .ttl and json-LD.

A few key concepts when working with SAP Knowledge Graph:
 
Resource Description Framework (RDF): Graph-like format utilizing the relationship between entities to connect information

RDF Triples

  1. Subject - The entity being described (e.g., a product, person, or organization).
  2. Predicate - The relationship between the subject and the object (e.g., "manufactures," "owns," "located in").
  3. Object - The value or entity linked to the subject (e.g., a location, another person, or an attribute).

RDF enables storing and querying relationships dynamically, making it a key foundation for knowledge graphs and AI-driven reasoning. Beneath SAP’s Business Data Cloud, semantics and ontologies are kept intact.

URI (Uniform Resource Identifier): Unique identifier for an entity or relationship within the knowledge graph.

This blog covers an integration of SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph with Gen AI Hub on Python, uploading and querying an RDF graph. For starters, refer to the SAP Discovery Center Mission –  Building Intelligent Data Applications with SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graphs.

 

Prerequisites

  • Access to SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph Engine
  • Access to SAP Generative AI Hub SDK (SAP AI Core on SAP BTP)
  • A Python environment

Step 1: Set up an SAP HANA Cloud instance where Triple Store is enabled

Within SAP HANA Database, enable ‘Triple Store’ on SAP HANA Cloud Central tool during instance provisioning

  • Step 5: Advanced Setting tab
  • Prerequisite: 3 vCPUs compute / 48 GB memory or more
  • Recommended: At least 8 vCPUs & more for enhanced performance

The following steps 2–10 uses Python programmatically.

Step 2: Create Knowledge Graphs (RDF format)

We start by loading RDF/XML file into an RDFLib graph. RDFLib is a Python library that supports the creation and manipulation of RDF triples programmatically. The RDF file used in this example refers to the open-source Financial Plan Statements – Cash Flow. The raw data contains many predicates, but for this use case, we will filter triples containing specific predicates like rowID, dscrpt (description), and infl_outfl (inflow/outflow).

Define namespaces matching the RDF predicates. Create a new graph to store filtered triples that contain only relevant predicates. This reduces data noise and keeps the RDF data focused on important attributes.

Finally, the filtered data is saved in .ttl format for ingestion into SAP HANA.

from rdflib import Graph, Namespace

g = Graph()
g.parse("rows.rdf", format="xml")

ns1 = Namespace("http://www.socrata.com/rdf/terms#")
ns2 = Namespace("https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/_4ubz-8kkx/")
filtered = Graph()
filtered.bind("ns1", ns1)
filtered.bind("ns2", ns2)

for s, p, o in g:
    if p in (ns1.rowID, ns2.dscrpt, ns2.infl_outfl):
        filtered.add((s, p, o))
filtered.serialize(destination="rows_filtered_100.ttl", format="turtle")

Step 3: Loading RDF Graph into SAP HANA Cloud

Using SAP HANA’s dbapi, the filtered RDF data is uploaded into a named RDF graph within SAP HANA Cloud via a stored procedure (SPARQL_EXECUTE). This makes the semantic data available for SPARQL querying within the database.

This integration enables leveraging SAP HANA’s graph engine and SPARQL query capabilities on the ingested RDF.

from hdbcli import dbapi

conn = dbapi.connect(user="USERNAME", password="PASSWORD", address="ADDRESS", port=443)
cursor = conn.cursor()

rdf_filename = "rows_filtered_100.ttl"
graphname = 'financial_plan_statements_cashflow_filtered_100'

with open(rdf_filename, 'r') as rdf_file:
    rdf_data = rdf_file.read()

request_hdrs = (
    'rqx-load-protocol: true\r\n'
    f'rqx-load-filename: {rdf_filename}\r\n'
    f'rqx-load-graphname: {graphname}\r\n'
)

cursor.callproc('SPARQL_EXECUTE', (rdf_data, request_hdrs, '', None))

Once ready, the filtered RDF graph is loaded into SAP HANA Cloud’s graph store. You can view the triples that you’ve uploaded via the HANA Cloud Central SQL Console. The SQL Console is configured to show a maximum number of 250,000 rows, configurable via settings.

 

Note: Under Instances, copy SQL Endpoint (top right) to use for HANA Cloud API connectivity.

Step 4: Setting Up Generative AI Hub SDK

The Generative AI Hub SDK provides an interface to LLMs.

To bridge natural language questions to RDF data queries, we configure an SAP AI Core client and proxy to use an LLM. In this case, anthropic—claude-3.5-sonnet is used. Do note you’ll have to deploy the model you’re using, and you can do so via SAP AI Launchpad.

from ai_core_sdk.ai_core_v2_client import AICoreV2Client
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.amazon import ChatBedrock
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client

def setup(): 
    proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')
    anthropic = ChatBedrock(
        model_name="anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet",
        proxy_client=proxy_client
    )

    # Connect to SAP HANA Cloud
    conn = dbapi.connect(
        user="USER",
        password="PASS",  # alternatively, set up os.environ 
        address=’',
        port=443,
    )

    return anthropic, conn

Step 5: Extracting Metadata from RDF via SPARQL

You need a structured understanding of your data before you can generate SQL queries. From SAP HANA Cloud, we extract RDF metadata triples relevant to user queries by executing SPARQL queries through the stored procedure interface. The results are parsed from XML into Python data structures. This metadata describes table schemas, columns, and relationships useful for downstream query construction.

def extract_metadata(question, conn):
    sparql_query = """
    SELECT ?s ?p ?o
    WHERE {
        ?s ?p ?o.
        FILTER(STRSTARTS(STR(?s), "https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/_4ubz-8kkx"))
    }
    """
    resp = cursor.callproc('SPARQL_EXECUTE', (sparql_query, 'metadata headers', '?', None))
    # Parse XML response to get triple metadata
    return metadata

Step 6: Analyzing Metadata with AI

The extracted metadata, combined with the user’s natural language question, is fed into Anthropic Claude (via Gen AI Hub SDK and LangChain integrations). A prompt template guides the LLM to identify tables, columns, filters, joins, and groupings needed for SQL generation.

The LLM understands complex RDF metadata and the user’s intent. It identifies the schema and specific SQL components required for query construction. This step bridges natural language understanding and formal query syntax.

def analyze_metadata(metadata, question, anthropic):
    # Reduce metadata to manageable size
    # Format metadata and question into prompt
    # Send to Anthropic Claude for structured analysis
    # Parse response into components: tables, columns, filters, joins, group_by
    return components

Step 7: Generating SQL Queries
SQL queries are dynamically constructed against the RDF graph exposed as a virtual table (SPARQL_TABLE). The generated SQL matches the SPARQL graph structure. Only valid columns (subject, predicate, object) are used. Filters and grouping are incorporated based on the prior analysis. The result is executable on SAP HANA Cloud.

def generate_sql(components):
    # Compose SELECT clause with allowed columns (subject, predicate, object)
    # Compose FROM clause using SPARQL_TABLE function for graph access
    # Apply WHERE filters and GROUP BY clauses as identified
    return sql_query

Step 8: Executing SQL and Returning Results

The constructed SQL is executed via the DB cursor. Results are structured in tabular form for easy further processing or presentation.

def execute_sql(sql_query, conn):
    cursor.execute(sql_query)
    rows = cursor.fetchall()
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=[desc[0] for desc in cursor.description])
    return df

Step 9: Generating Natural Language Responses

Finally, the SQL query results are fed back into the LLM to generate a concise and coherent natural language answer. 

def generate_response_structured(question, results, anthropic):
    prompt = f"""
    Convert the following query results into a natural language response...
    Question: {question}
    Results: {results.to_string()}
    """
    response = anthropic.invoke(prompt)
    return response.content

Step 10: End-to-End Query Processing

The whole pipeline ties together in this step by:

  • Extracting metadata
  • Analyzing metadata
  • Generating SQL query
  • Executing SQL query
  • Producing natural language results from the executed SQL query
def process_question(question, conn, anthropic):
    metadata = extract_metadata(question, conn)
    components = analyze_metadata(metadata, question, anthropic)
    sql_query = generate_sql(components)
    results = execute_sql(sql_query, conn)
    response = generate_response_structured(question, results, anthropic)
return response

The output, adjusted to show only natural language: 

This end-to-end system enables users to ask complex questions over RDF graphs stored in SAP HANA Cloud — all with natural language.

This pipeline showcases a powerful synergy of

  • Semantic data managed with RDF and SPARQL
  • Enterprise-grade persistence and querying on SAP HANA Cloud
  • Generative AI that interprets user intent and translates it into precise data queries
  • Natural language explanations that make complex data accessible

This approach unlocks AI-assisted analytics and querying on complex RDF datasets, suitable for financial statements, geographical data, and more—delivering real-time insights.

Leave any comments below!

entry.title: Re: Cash and Liquidity Management: Memo Records
entry.content: Hi @SilviaCienfuegos1988 , seems like you are first creating a forecast flow and then an ACTUAL flow. You can use transaction FCLM_CR_INTRAM or report FCLM_CR_TRIGGER, which will reconcile actual and forecast flows. Refer KBA 3373339.

entry.title: Creating the Shell System for SAP Business Transformation Center: A Practical Approach
entry.content:
Lean Selective Data Transition (Lean SDT) with SAP Business Transformation Center (BTC) supports the transition of data that is initially analyzed and reduced to the amount that is relevant for continuing business in the new SAP S/4HANA system.

To use this transition path, a so-called shell copy of the source SAP ECC system is required — containing only customizing without application data, such as master or transactional data. This shell system is then converted to at least SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition.

In this blog, I will provide initial insights into the shell creation process and illustrate it with a practical example.

General Approach

A copy of the source system is required to create the shell (1). This can be achieved using virtualization, third-party tools, or — as in my case — a classical system copy.

Once the system copy is complete, the client designated for SAP Business Transformation Center is deleted, recreated, and copied using a client copy with a customizing-only profile (2).

Let’s take a closer look at this approach.

Initial Situation

In this example, I used an SAP ERP 6.0 EhP8 system with the System ID (SID) SRC as the source system. It contained transactional data in client 300 and was running on an AnyDB database.

To simplify the process and reduce the number of steps involved, I opted for a heterogeneous system copy, migrating directly to an SAP HANA database. The migration key required for my customer number had already been generated in advance.

For the client copy, I performed a remote client copy. Depending on the scenario, a local client copy or client export/import could also be valid alternatives.

DISCLAIMER: 
This blog shows selected steps tailored to my specific setup. When creating your own shell system, you should always follow the official SAP documentation for System Copy and Client Copy procedures, and prepare your environment accordingly. Depending on the size of the system or the connected systems, it may be advisable to consider an alternative option.

Step 1: Generate DDL Statements (Source)

To prepare for the migration of non-standard database objects, I first logged into the source system (client 000), opened transaction SE38, and executed the report SMIGR_CREATE_DDL.

In the dialog, I selected SAP HANA Database as the target and specified the installation directory before running the report.

The DDL statements have been successfully generated and stored in the specified directory.



Step 2: Export the Database Instance (Source)

First, I copied, extracted, and launched the Software Provisioning Manager (SWPM) on the source system.

On the Welcome screen, I navigated to: SAP Business Suite 7i 2016 > EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 ABAP > {AnyDB} > System Copy > Source System > Based on AS ABAP and then selected the Database Instance Export option.

I followed the wizard, providing the necessary inputs, such as the Export Location.

As the Target Database Type, I selected SAP HANA Database.

Next, I supplied the SQL files generated in Step 1.

Using mostly default settings, I reviewed the summary and started the export process.

After some time, the procedure completed successfully without any issues.

Finally, I copied the database export to the shell system.

Step 3: Install the Target System (Shell)

First, I transferred the Software Provisioning Manager and required installation media (SAP Kernel, SAP IGS and helper applications, SAP Host Agent, SAP HANA database and client, ...) to the shell system. I then extracted and launched the Software Provisioning Manager.

On the Welcome screen, I navigated to: SAP Business Suite 7i 2016 > EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 ABAP > SAP HANA Database > System Copy > Target System > Standard System > Based on AS ABAP and then selected the Standard System installation option.

I followed the wizard, retaining the same System ID (SID) as for the source system.

ADDITIONAL INFO: 
It is also possible to use a new System ID. In this specific example, however, I chose to retain the original System ID.

Next, I used the Standard System Copy / Migration (Load-Based) option.

Since the SAP HANA database was not pre-installed on the host, I configured the Software Provisioning Manager to install it as part of the standard system installation. To enable the installation, I specified localhost as the Database Host and changed the Database ID (DBSID) to HDB.

Next, I specified the Migration Export (MIGEXPORT) copied earlier from the source system.

Using mostly default settings, I reviewed the summary and started the system installation.

After some time, the procedure completed successfully without any issues. I completed the required post-processing tasks, including disabling the SAP Cloud ALM push provider, as it is not required for this system.



Step 4: Delete the Client (Shell)

Before running the client copy with the customizing profile, it is necessary to delete the existing client 300 on the shell system.

To do this, I temporarily enabled the SAP* user (login/no_automatic_user_sapstar = false) on the shell system, logged into client 300 as SAP*, and opened transaction SCC5.

The client was already pre-selected. I enabled the Delete entry from T000 checkbox and scheduled the job to run immediately in the background.

In transaction SCC3, I monitored the deletion process until it was completed.



Step 5: Recreate the Client (Shell)

As a next step, it is required to create the client 300 again in the shell system.

I logged into client 000, opened transaction SCC4, switched to Edit mode, and created the new client 300 using New Entries.

After saving, the new client was successfully created.



Step 6: Perform Remote Client Copy (Shell)

With the new client in place, I logged into client 300 as the SAP* user. I then created an RFC destination (type 3) to the source system, using the Fast serializer as a special option (CLIENTCOPY_SRCCLNT300).

In transaction SCC9, I configured a remote client copy using the profile SAP_UCSV (Customizing, User Master Records and User Variants), selected the defined RFC destination, and scheduled the job to run immediately in the background. The customizing data began to transfer to the shell system.

ADDITIONAL INFO: 
It is possible to use other customizing profiles instead of SAP_UCSV. In this example, I explicitly chose to include user master records and user variants.

Using transaction SCC3, I monitored the remote copy process until it was completed.

The shell system has been successfully created.

Summary & Next Steps

I hope, with this blog, I was able to share initial insights on the shell copy with you and provide you with a practical guide to create the system for SAP Business Transformation Center. When creating the shell copy yourself, it is essential to carefully plan your scenario and select the option that best fits your specific system landscape and project requirements.

With the shell system successfully created, it can now be converted using the standard path to SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition. In my case, I proceeded first with Custom Code Migration, created the Deletion Transport Request, and then converted the system to SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS03.

entry.title: SAP IAS and Successfactors - How SAML2 Attributes are used
entry.content:
Hi

I created this post for better understanding how it works. It isn't a post how to configure/fix errors. Just for better understanding of process. Our configuration works, but in some special cases I would like to extend my knowledge about.

We have a situation
SF is integrated with IAS and IAS is federated with CorporateIDP EntraID (no enriched an so one) 

On Entra ID side we have a configuration 

 

UPN has format of the email address and is used as the login in EntraID
but some partners have own Email outside our domain as on example below

We have a SF app - standard configuration delivered by SAP 

with standard attributes 

App is federated with CorporateIDP

User in SF and IAS had following settings

Same login name and mail as on SF side 

But user wasn't able to logon to SF (IAS/EntraID logon was ok)

In IAS log we had 

Of course, after changing email on IAS side from first.last@domain.pl to username@partner.com logon to SF is possible,


Looking in the documentation (maybe I do not understand whole process correctly) 
 

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/corp-idp-configure-identity-federation?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/configure-identity-federation-for-applications

When Use Identity Authentication user store option is enabled, the application checks if the users authenticated by the corporate identity provider exist in the Identity Directory, the user store of Identity Authentication. The existence check is done with the name identifier sent by the corporate identity provider for the identifying attributes uid, loginName, emailsand phoneNumber.

For users that exist in the Identity Directory, data from the user store is taken and the subject name identifier, assertion and default attributes according to the application configuration are sent. For users with no profile in Identity Authentication, the application receives the nameID attribute from the corporate IdP assertion, and the attributes according to the application configuration.

 

In my understanding, if federation is enabled, attributes should be taken from user record in IAS. But by some reasons mail from CorporateIDP is used.
Question why? 

Thanks for any suggestions & feedback 🙂
Artur

entry.title: Re: Empowering business to manage Decisions in SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
@nsathya @MioYasutake : For your query on where this app can be enabled in the realm of multi-system landscape (dev, test, prod, etc.) there is no single answer. It depends on our customer data, the rule itself, and the authorization concepts that are in place. This application can be used in any of the system where an user has access to edit the project.

Business users can use this application in non-production system (ex., dev, test or pre-prod), make the changes and deploy. IT team can then transport it to the Production tenant. This setup works when the data maintained in the rule is consistent across the landscape. If the rule (ex., decision table) is configured to use client-specific data like customer details, payment terms, etc. then one would not want that data replicated to production. Plus, there is also an additional step of transporting the content from non-prod to prod system.

User can directly modify the data in tenant used for productive purposes, if ideally the below conditions are met. It comes with its advantages that the changes are in production as soon as the project was deployed.

  • The tenant type is not marked as production in SAP Build control tower. This allows ALL project and its artefacts to be editable.
  • Since all the projects are editable, you would want to have a robust roles and authentication on who can access what. Giving appropriate roles to users and adding right authorization for the users at project-level is needed. Equally important is the maintenance of these roles and authorization.
  • The use case requires the business users changes are immediately reflected in the production system.

One additional note: If you want the updated decision to be used by a running process, then a change in the project setup is needed. I will write a separate blog about this.

entry.title: Re: GSTR1 report: In Run Statutory reports taxitems showi...
entry.content:
Hi, 

 

Please note, generated taxitems (GSTR1 report) are for reference to showing the result of sections and documents reporting to the period in the run statutory reports app.

 

In 'Manage customer line items' app line items are showing the amount with separated with debit and credit column. Debit means '-' and credit means '+' symbol.

 

Once the taxitems are generated, the corresponding amounts—such as Base Amount and CGST Amount—are shown with appropriate currency symbols in the Run Statutory Reports application.

 

Please refer the below help document for more information related to GSTR1 report,

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/634261119fec4d58970471f2c4a9a740/c8289d53d3294345bd1a6d98f84e70ef.html?locale=en-US

 

Best regards,

Atanu

SAP Support - S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: Strengthening SAP Security: A Closer Look at Authorizations and Access Control
entry.content:
As promised in my previous post , it's time to explore the different domains of the SAP Secure Operations Map (SOM). This series aims to highlight key areas that deserve attention and showcase tools and services that can support your company on its journey toward a more secure SAP environment.

We'll begin with a topic that most professionals associate as the cornerstone of SAP security: Authorizations and Access Control.

The cornerstone of SAP Security: Authorizations and Access Control

The Challenge: Keeping Authorization Concepts Current

One of the biggest pain points in this domain is that authorization concepts often become outdated almost immediately after implementation. You may have defined a comprehensive concept at a certain point in time, but if your access control processes are not actively maintained and updated, that concept quickly loses its relevance.

This issue is especially prevalent in ERP systems, which offer a vast range of highly specific authorization options. While SaaS applications may be less complex in this regard, challenges still arise—particularly when defining business roles that span multiple systems. Consider a purchaser who uses S/4HANA for procurement, SAP Ariba for supplier collaboration, and SAP Analytics Cloud for reporting. Understanding and managing the authorization requirements across such a landscape is far from straightforward.

Risks and Red Flags

Proper access control isn't just about functionality—it’s about minimizing risk. For example:

  • Critical Authorizations: Users should not have direct access to sensitive system elements such as table contents unless absolutely necessary.
  • Segregation of Duties (SoD): A single user should not have the ability to execute multiple critical steps in a process. For instance, allowing a purchaser to both create purchase orders and maintain vendor master data could open the door to fraud.

Where to Begin? Start with Your Access Control Processes!

To build a resilient access control framework, begin by asking key questions about your current processes:

  • How are access control tasks handled today?
  • Are access risk definitions actively managed?
  • How are roles assigned—manually or through tools?
  • Are authorizations maintained regularly?
  • Are there ongoing checks for SoD (segregation of duties) conflicts?
  • Do users only retain appropriate authorizations when their roles or positions change?
  • How are privileged operations (e.g., "firefighter access") managed?

Once you’ve answered these questions and established clear processes, you can explore opportunities for optimization—such as automation or dedicated tools like SAP Access Control and SAP Identity Access Governance (IAG).

Begin by asking key questions about your current access control processes

Role Optimization and Reducing Privilege Creep

As part of your access control maturity, role optimization is a logical next step. If your processes haven’t been streamlined in the past, it’s likely that "privilege creep"—the gradual accumulation of excessive authorizations—has taken hold.

One powerful tool based service to support role optimization is AIRC ("Automated Implementation of Role Concept"). AIRC generates role proposals based on actual system usage, helping you accelerate the development of a new role concept. With AIRC, time to value can be as short as three months. For more details, take a look at this informative blog post by Markus: Optimizing Your SAP S/4HANA Landscape with a Best Practice Role Concept

AIRC can also support your SAP BTP authorization journey. Learn more about that here: Authorizations for SAP Business Technology Platform – A Quick Start

Need Support?

If you need help setting up or optimizing your access control processes, we’re here to support you.

Next up, for the next post in this series, where I’ll take a deeper look at SAP Access Control and SAP IAG—and how they can be effectively leveraged across a hybrid SAP landscape.

entry.title: Next Inspection Date not updated during 301 movement – Inspection Lot 09 not created automatically
entry.content:
Hello colleagues,

I’m encountering an issue in SAP QM where the Next Inspection Date is not automatically updated in the batch master (transaction MSC2N) after performing a 301 movement type (stock transfer). Because of this, the system does not automatically create the inspection lot of type 09, even though the lot can be created manually once the date is entered.

Details:

The inspection lot 09 is created, but only if the Next Inspection Date is already present in the batch.

With movement type 301, the Next Inspection Date is not populated, so the lot is not created automatically.

If I manually maintain the Next Inspection Date in MSC2N, the inspection lot 09 is then created correctly.

With movement type 501, the Next Inspection Date is filled automatically, and inspection lot 09 is created as expected.

Inspection types 08 and 09 are active and properly configured in the material master, including the “automatic assignment” flag for type 09.

My questions are:

Is it standard behavior that movement type 301 does not update the Next Inspection Date?

How can I automatically fill the Next Inspection Date after a 301 movement, to ensure automatic creation of inspection lot type 09?

 

Any insights or experience would be greatly appreciated.
Thank you!

entry.title: Blog It Forward - Marco Büscher 2025 Edition
entry.content:
Thank you for the opportunity to give a friendly introduction in the form of the BIF format. My name is Marco Büscher, and I am a passionate SAP consultant with a deep enthusiasm for all things Man at the SAP World Tour 2010 in HannoverSAP technologies. Even when I immersed myself in the world of enterprise software in the field of MM in 2009, I was fascinated by the possibilities that SAP offers to optimize business processes and make companies more efficient.

SAP training material

I have now spent the last 15+ years learning MM, PP, ABAP, SD, CO, and FI (on the stack on the left, the training documents for sales and controlling are still missing, but these have now been digitized in the SAP Learning HUB to save paper and costs). Each of these modules not only provided me with technical know-how but also taught me the importance of understanding the needs and challenges of users.  But, I think you can all agree with me on one fact: having a lot of good capabilities on PowerPoint slides explaining to you what could happen is nice but will not do the job, and that's what we're looking for, especially when it comes to transformation. I am often asked, What are you doing? And I say, we are NTT DATA, one of the biggest consulting companies in the world. Our mission statement is NTT DATA uses information technology to create new paradigms and value that help contribute to a more affluent and harmonious society. In simpler terms, NTT DATA aims to leverage technology to improve society and create value for its clients and the world. And that is, for me, the way, and I go with SAP…  So we have three tasks. First, we will do our utmost to serve our customers. Second, we will meet the challenges of our work with an active and positive spirit. Third, we will create a lively and vibrant company. So, SAP was already working in real-time in the 70s, which meant that SAP systems were able to process and deliver data in real-time, which was an incredible step forward and only added to my fascination with SAP. We consultants, for example, look at a seven-year timeframe because we are concerned with short-term developments at SAP, and we look at the seven-year period, and we expect seven to nineteen years and more in terms of skills, development, and overall planning. I believe that real solutions and KI are not only about code and technical skills but also about empathy and the ability to communicate with and lead people. So what I am really after is getting the full picture of the Business and acting better and getting better effects... I enjoy working in a dynamic environment where I am constantly challenged to find new solutions and collaborate with different teams. In every new challenge, be it a complex project or a new customer contact, I see an opportunity to learn and grow.  My goal is to be there for my customers, not only as a consultant or developer but also as a partner at NTT DATA — someone who understands them and helps them achieve their goals.

Years ago, we learned about SAP innovations at CeBIT in Hanover, and I sometimes go there. There were new features and innovations to see, as well as artifacts from the history of SAP development. Among other things, an illustration of one of the punch cards that SAP had created for ICI from the SAP timeline. Punched cards are a historical medium for data storage and processing. They usually consist of thick cardboard or plastic cards with holes in certain positions. These holes represent binary data (0 and 1) and made it possible to store information such as machine code or data.

People at the SAP stand at Cebit 2010 in HannoverMotivation at the SAP stand at Cebit 2010 in HannoverSAP Cargo Scenario with SAP HANA

Man on one of the first bikes with Bluetooth at SAP

 

 

 

 

 

 

Man in front of the SAP Timeline8-bit system for mainframe computers and punched cards

SAP Codejam´s

Participants SAP CodeJam 2023 in Heilbronn

From my point of view in a role as an SAP consultant and developer, I always strive to actively share knowledge and foster a community of SAP enthusiasts. For this reason, I am committed to organizing SAP CodeJams throughout Germany, for example, to see what the future holds for us because if you make the mistake, which is quite often made, that you just look at what the operations hold for you today and what you plan to have today, you always prepare for the business of yesterday. Participants SAP CodeJam 2019 in Lingen(EMS)These events not only offer an excellent platform to enter into dialogue with colleagues and interested developers, but also the opportunity to learn together and develop innovative ideas. For me, this is not only about sharing knowledge but also about strengthening the team spirit and creating a creative space where we can work together on agile solutions to complex challenges. Because every Company has the same problem: limited recources of people so with that, how do we do it in practical terms?

 

SAP Meetup´s

I also really enjoy bringing people together—whether at informal meetings such as regulars' tables or in small groups—where they can present their projects, exchange ideas, and inspire each other through open and meaningful discussions.Speaker at an SAP regulars' table in Lingen 2017

 

 

SAP Education

Wall in the SAP building Ratingen 2018

So what has changed over time is as well again from a SAP Consultants Point of view, is learning and certifying in SAP modules. It used to be a very different experience. In Ratingen i.e. a popular training location, professionals gathered in classrooms to delve deeply into the complex facets of SAP. The atmosphere was characterized by a mixture of curiosity and engagement, while trainers delved deep into the content and encouraged participants to actively participate.

Questions were discussed, solutions debated and practical exercises carried out in front of the large screens and whiteboards. It was a time when personal interaction and direct exchange with trainers and other participants enriched the learning environment and deepened understanding of the software. The diversity of perspectives and experiences of those present helped to provide a broader picture of the SAP world. So what i am really after is getting the full picture, getting a full synchronization having better picture and acting better to develop better solutions. This type of learning not only created knowledge but also a network of contacts that lasted beyond the training. With the shift to the digital world, the framework conditions may have changed, but the desire to continuously develop and learn remains regardless of the format.

The memories of these intensive training days, even though they were conducted by an SAP training partner, are characterized by a sense of comRoom allocation plan for the Zerti in the SAP building Ratingen 2018munity, which was not only about individual efforts but also about teamwork and mutual support. Just let me also stress one point: the opportunity to ask personal questions directly to experienced trainers or colleagues and to learn in a dynamic group fostered a deep understanding of the subject was profitable for all, and that's the point where also the SAP Community came in. Here we can have a community that is open-minded to get in communication with others. Even though virtual training has now taken precedence, the desire for these personal experiences and face-to-face exchanges remains alive, as they formed a valuable foundation for many in their professional development.

 

Visit to Walldorf

The SAP Logo and spring fountain in WalldorfAnother highlight of my trip was undoubtedly the visit to the SAP headquarters in Walldorf. As I entered the modern buildings, I immediately felt the electrifying atmosphere that surrounded the place. Every element seemed to reflect the innovative power that SAP is known for. Talking to Svea Becker, the SAP Community Advocate, the passion and vision behind SAP's work became even clearer to me.

I was particularly impressed by the ongoing renovation work on the buildings, which are around 30 years old. This measure shows SAP's ambition to continuously renew itself and remain attractive to young talent. It is not just a question of aesthetic appearance, but also a sign of appreciation for the creative minds Svea Becker, SAP Advocate at SAP and me in Walldorf who will be working in these modern premises. The company wants to show that there is a place for innovation and fresh ideas - and this is reflected in the modern architecture that I was able to admire. 

Another fascinating part of my visit was the walk through the Zen garden. This tranquil oasis not only provided a contrast to the hustle and bustle of the offices, but also symbolized the open corporate culture that SAP stands for. In this harmonious setting, Svea and I talked about the various initiatives SAP has launched to strengthen the community. At this place I can feel that SAP works for Customers and Partners and not against them... The People have a lot of engagement for their Job... 

Svea shed light on the challenges facing the community such as the fast-moving technology landscape and the need to constantly adapt. Her stories about the strategies SAP is pursuing to overcome these hurMan in front of the SAP International Training Center Walldorfdles were both insightful and inspiring. She conveyed a sense of confidence that, despite the difficulties, collaboration and sharing within the community can lead to real progress. Finally, we had the opportunity to take a look at the international training center. The visit to Walldorf will remain in my memory not only as a professional highlight but also as a profound experience that shaped my perspective on the importance of community and collaboration in the technology industry. It was a trip that not only opened up new perspectives for me but also highlighted the unmistakable power of networking and shared learning.

 

 

Fun facts about me

Golf ball and golf club

Golfers on tee 1Taking part in golf not only offers the opportunity to relax and network but also teaches a variety of skills that are of great importance in the professional world. Golf is a game of strategy and strategic thinking. Every shot requires careful consideration—from choosing the right club to assessing the course conditions. These skills are equally important in the business world, where decisions often have far-reaching consequences and well-thought-out strategies can make the difference between success and failure.

In addition, golf is a school of patience. Success on the course is rarely immediate; rather, it requires repeated practice and a willingness to learn from setbacks. This perseverance, coupled with the ability to take the long view, is invaluable in many professions, especially those involving long-term projects or complex tasks.

"How to Play Golf" | A Classic Mickey Cartoon

Another important aspect of the game of golf is the value of fair play. Etiquette and respectful interaction on the golf course foster a sense of integrity that is also important in the business world. Fairness and recognizing the contributions of others are critical to building trust and creating a positive team dynamic.

 

 

 

Describe in a short sentence: "What needs does your SAP solution meet?

Our SAP solutions meet the need for seamlessly integrated, automated, and flexible business processes that enable real-time data analysis and improve efficiency and decision-making in organizations.

 

What 5 things do you really want to achieve in life?

Family and relationships: Build and maintain strong, loving relationships with family and friends. Travel and cultural experiences: Explore the world, learn about different cultures, and have unforgettable travel experiences. Personal development: Constant learning and personal development through education, new hobbies, and self-reflection. Financial independence: Build a secure financial foundation to minimize future uncertainty. Making a positive impact: acting in the community to help others and bring about positive social change.

 

What do you envision or predict for the future of your topic? (Mobile, cloud, etc.—be creative, be brave, be entertaining.)

Human Interface 2.0: Imagine a world where your thoughts are transformed directly into the cloud to share information or decisions in real-time. Brain-computer interfaces could act as the new smartphone—simple, and the data flows!

Emotional AI assistants: Our mobile devices could develop a higher level of emotional understanding and support us as personal life coaches. They would be able to recognize our mood and make appropriate suggestions for activities, music, or conversations to promote our well-being.

 

If your work gave you a whole day every week to do whatever you feel like doing, what would it be?

If I had a whole day every week to do whatever I wanted, I would spend it playing golf. I love being outdoors, and there’s something special about the fresh air on the course. I’d focus on my swing, strategize my next moves, and enjoy the game. It’s also a great way to hang out with friends or meet new people. Whether I’m practicing my drives, working on my putting, or just soaking in the beautiful scenery, a day of golf would be relaxing and fun!

 

What was the funniest project you have ever participated in, and why?

We were called to a two-week project, and during this time the project manager had taken vacation.

 

Completion

If you find yourself in one or the other situation, please leave a Kudo... 🙂

I would like to pass the blog on to my colleague @Hilal_Kurt from Turkey, who has just become an SAP Champion after many years of working with the SAP community. I am sure she has many interesting things to share with us.

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2505
entry.content:
SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2505

With the introduction of the new release 2505, this blog post presents a comprehensive overview of the latest features and enhancements. The release has been available since May 17th and serves as the successor to the 2502 release. Some highlights of the 2505 release include the reduction of the technical downtime for release upgrades, the quick Fiori app generation within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) and the AI-based RAP BO Generator. 

The list of all new features can be found on the What's New for SAP BTP ABAP environment page. 

All new AI features are summarized here.

 

ABAP Development Tools (ADT)

  • Working with the log when migrating CDS DDIC-based views to CDS view entities, after finishing the simulation or migration of CDS DDIC-based views to CDS view entities, you can now filter the log on the result page using the Show Objects drop-down list box 
  • Restructuring the "Code Completion" and "Source Code Editors" preference pages 
  • RAP Business Objects Test Double Framework: Supported EML Statements
  • Data preview now supports ‘between’ capabilities in the filter options
  • Testing web API-based services using Swagger UI
  • Creating SAP Fiori App using Quick Fiori Application Generator
  • Improvements in the ABAP coverage view, new features: Changing the coverage scope and Exclude from coverage 
  • ADT Links can now be incorporated in the HTML result of an IDE action, allowing users to navigate to development objects in ADT 
  • ABAP SQL Consumption Model, you can it to generate corresponding CDS external entities for each entity that is exposed in a SQL service 
  • Creating and editing CDS table entities 

Core Data Services (CDS)

  • Improved CDS Buffering, optimize the ABAP memory consumption for scenarios which make use of CDS Entity Buffering 

OData

  • OData UI Service from Scratch, you can create all RAP-service related repository objects from scratch by using the OData UI Service from Scratch wizard (details)
  • Null value handling, you can now find an overview of the differences in the behavior of null values and initial values in OData V2 and V4 with regard to sorting and filtering on the Fiori Elements UI as well as explicit filtering through OData Web API request (details)

Data Archiving

  • Storage Manager Implementation, you can now use a new interface method to rollback faulty verified archive files (details)

Application Jobs

  • New Skip Function, you can now skip a single execution of a periodic job chain in the Application Job Templates app (details)

Units of measurement

  • You can now retrieve the standard units of measurement and dimensions delivered by SAP using released ABAP Cloud APIs (CL_UOM*) (details)

Business Configuration App

  • New Email Configuration for the Upload Business Configuration App (details)
  • Intent Navigation, if all technical key fields are provided as parameters to uniquely identify a record of an object page or subobject page, it's opened directly (details)

XCO Library

  • XCO XSLX: You can now style the fonts of a cell of an Excel worksheet (details)

Print Queue Administrator Integration

  • You can now create and delete print queues directly using APIs without the need to enter the Maintain Print Queues app, you can do so using the communication scenario SAP_COM_0A86 (details)

IAM Information System

  • You can now display the relevant components for business catalogs and applications. This way you can check under which component you can create an incident if required (details)

Released APIs and software component relations

  • You can now read about the best practices of using released APIs and software component relations on the SAP Help Portal (details)

Provider Support Role

  • Include start authorizations for custom services in provider support role 

Development Test Role

  • The business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_EXT_TST for development test roles is now continuously extended, not only with the start authorizations for custom services, but also with full authorizations for any custom authorization object included in the authorization default values 

Technical Monitoring

  • Capture and download of PLV files in ABAP cross trace for SQL statement execution analysis (details)

Message Monitoring

  • Message Monitoring for Integration Experts, you can now download the original payload of your message (details)

Custom Code Analysis

  • 3-Tier Model ATC Checks, there are now two new ATC checks of category 3-Tier Model: "Usage of APIs" and "Allowed Enhancement Technologies" (details)

Landscape Portal

  • Monitoring customers and products, the new Customer Overview app in the Landscape Portal enables partners to keep track of their customers and products (details)

SAP Fiori Launchpad

  • Extend the Sort Priority Field in the Manage Launchpad Spaces App, you can now extend the sort priority field to +/- 9999.99999 (details)
  • Avatars for Themes in the User Settings (details)
  • Hide Empty Spaces and Pages in the SAP Fiori Launchpad (details)

SAPUI5

  • Referenced SAPUI5 Version, as part of hotfix collection 3,  SAPUI5 version 1.133.2 is used for the administrator’s SAP Fiori launchpad, SAP-delivered Fiori apps, and the custom-built SAP Fiori apps, which are deployed to the ABAP system (see upgrade windows and SAPUI5)

SAP HANA Cloud

  • Increased SAP HANA Cloud Backup Retention Time to 30 Days (details)

entry.title: SAP CAP BTP Kibana configuration Log
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am developing CAP applications with Java on SAP BTP. On BTP, I have access to Kibana, and to enable logging in Kibana I added the following to my mta.yaml:

I am facing two issues:

- name: maycap-logging
type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
parameters:
service: application-logs
service-plan: lite

This module seems to be deprecated, and I am not sure what the recommended alternative is for logging on BTP.
The logs are not well formatted: error messages are hard to read, and the stack traces are not as clear as I am used to from SAP Commerce, where the full stack trace is visible. Also, in SAP Commerce, when I use the org.slf4j.MDC library, I can easily see the tags I add in the logs, but with CAP on BTP this does not seem to work.
Additionally, I have searched for a configuration guide for logging with CAP Java applications on BTP, but I have not been able to find one.

How can I resolve these issues?
Is there a recommended way to configure logging for CAP Java applications on BTP, so that logs (including MDC tags and stack traces) are clearly visible in Kibana?

Thank you for your help!

entry.title: Is 'Price per User per Month' Model Possible in SAP ByDesign Contracts?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

We sell subscriptions to our customers based on a price per user per month model.

For example, the unit price could be €2.62 per user per month for a given service. A single contract can include multiple services, each potentially with a different number of users. Importantly, the number of users can vary each month, and for each contract line within the same contract.

However, it seems that SAP ByDesign does not support this model, which is surprising to us — this type of recurring, usage-based billing seems like a basic and common use case.

From what we've understood, the "Quantity" field on contract lines can be used in two ways, but neither fits our needs:

  1. Quantity as a physical unit (e.g. EA = each):
    This can represent the number of users, but in this case, SAP interprets the unit price as applying to the entire contract duration, and there is no built-in way to specify that this price should be billed monthly.
  2. Quantity as a time unit (e.g. Month):
    If we define the quantity as a number of months and check the “Price by Period” option, the system correctly understands that the price should be billed monthly. However, in this case, we lose the link to the actual number of users, so there’s no way to multiply a “price per user per month” by the number of users.

This creates a confusing limitation, since our requirement seems simple:
We want to tell SAP ByDesign that:

  • The quantity is the number of users (which may change monthly),
  • The unit price is per user per month,
  • The system should bill that amount every month from the contract start date to the end date (or per contract line).

Are we missing something? Has anyone found a solution or workaround for this type of scenario?

Thanks in advance for your help.

AB

entry.title: Re: SAP IAS and Successfactors - How SAML2 Attributes are...
entry.content:
Hi

You're missing one piece here and is the configuration of your corporate IDP to IAS settings.

I guess you have that configured with the email attribute. You need to think that first the authentication goes from EntraID to IAS and here is where you need an attribute to match between your EntraID and your IAS. Based on that then IAS applies Federation and enrichs the user attributes with what you have in IAS. But if you're using one email in EntraID and another email in IAS and that's the Subject name identified, how is IAS supposed to know you're talking about the same user?

When there's no user in IAS to match and based on the claim names probably you're passing to SF your EntraID attributes. Remember the federation is intended to enrich attributes and IAS allows you to chose between IDP Attributes or IAS attributes to be sent to SF.

The best way if you're using SAML is to use the SAML-tracer tool or similar that can be installed in chrome/mozilla to check the saml messages one you logon and the attributes that are negotiated between EntraID-IAS-SF.

As a hint if your emails in SF (same as IAS since you're using IPS) are not the same as your emails in EntraID then the email is not the proper attribute as a subject name identifier for your trust IAS-EntraID. In one scenario we've used the Login name That matched with a specific atribute in EntraID.

entry.title: Re: SAP SuccessFactors WorkZone Page Load Times
entry.content:
Hi @Sebastian_W,

We go live with our WZ site in a week and like @arsalali pointed out, we saw the biggest lag time with the one render manager call. We've had a ticket open for the last couple of months and have been working with SAP to try to improve performance. They did make one update to the servers a couple of months ago that helped a little but, without caching, we still see 10 seconds or more to load. We have also been told that, for a non-cached version of the site, 10 seconds is about the expectation. Once cached, we do see improvements and pages take under 5 seconds usually. Wish I had better info to share, and I hope SAP continues to make improvements.

Regards,

John

entry.title: Re: Commodity Code
entry.content:
Hi @Anastasia80 ,

You can assign a different numbering scheme to each country in "Assign Numbering Scheme for Commodity Codes to Country/Region" activity. Then, in the Fiori app "Classify products - commodity codes", you can assign different commodity codes to the same material in each numbering scheme.

Best regards
